preamble: The shift from the Semitic pharyngeal fricative [ʕ] (Ayin) to the Greek vowel [o] (Omicron) is a foundational moment in the history of the alphabet. When the Greeks adapted the Phoenician abjad, they encountered guttural consonants absent from their phonology. The acoustic 'rounding' or lowering effect of the Ayin led Greek ears to interpret it as the vowel [o].

examples.note: The Greek letter Omicron was created by repurposing the Phoenician pharyngeal consonant ayin, whose acoustic backness was heard as [o] by Greek speakers.
The internal ayin was elided; the surrounding vowels merged. This example shows ain→∅ rather than a clean ain→o shift.

sources: Woodard, R. D. (1997). Greek Writing from Knossos to Homer. https://academic.oup.com/book/46905
